questions.question:
Let \(\Sigma = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}\). For \(x \in \Sigma^*\), let \(\text{prod}(x)\) be the product of symbols in \(x\) modulo 7. We take \(\text{prod}(\epsilon) = 1\), where \(\epsilon\) is the null string.

For example, \(\text{prod}(124) = (1 \times 2 \times 4) \mod 7 = 1\).

Define \( L = \{x \in \Sigma^* \mid \text{prod}(x) = 2\}. \)

The number of states in a minimum state DFA for \(L\) is ___________. (Answer in integer)

(GATE CSE 2025 SET-2)
questions.explanation: Click here for detail solution by gateoverflow

questions.question:
Let \(\Sigma = \{a, b, c\} \). For \(x \in \Sigma^* \), and \(\alpha \in \Sigma \), let \(\# _\alpha (x) \) denote the number of occurrences of \(\alpha \) in \(x \).

Which one or more of the following option(s) define(s) regular language(s)?

(GATE CSE 2025 SET-2)
options:
B.

\(\{ a, b \}^* \cap \{ a^m b^n c^{m-n} \mid m \geq n \geq 0 \} \)
questions.explanation: Click here for detail solution by gateoverflow

questions.question:
Consider two grammars \(G1 \) and \(G2 \) with the production rules given below:

\( G1: S \to \text{if} E \text{ then } S \mid \text{if} E \text{ then } S \text{ else } S \mid a \)
\( E \to b \)

\( G2: S \to \text{if} E \text{ then } S \mid M \)
\( M \to \text{if} E \text{ then } M \text{ else } S \mid c \)
\( E \to b \)

where \( if,then,else,a,b,c\) are the terminals.

Which of the following option(s) is/are CORRECT?

(GATE CSE 2025 SET-2)
options:
A.

\(G1 \) is not LL(1) and \(G2 \) is LL(1).

B.

\(G1 \) is LL(1) and \(G2 \) is not LL(1).
questions.explanation: Click here for detail solution by gateoverflow

questions.question:
Given a Context-Free Grammar \(G \) as follows:

\( S \to A a \mid b A c \mid d c \mid b d a \)
\( A \to d \)

Which ONE of the following statements is TRUE?

(GATE CSE 2025 SET-2)
questions.explanation: Click here for detail solution by gateoverflow

questions.question:
Consider the two lists List I and List II given below:
\( \begin{array}{|c l|c l|} \hline \textbf{List I} & & \textbf{List II} & \\ \hline \text{(i)} & \text{Context free languages} & \text{(a)} & \text{Closed under union} \\ \hline \text{(ii)} & \text{Recursive languages} & \text{(b)} & \text{Not closed under complementation} \\ \hline \text{(iii)} & \text{Regular languages} & \text{(c)} & \text{Closed under intersection} \\ \hline \end{array}\)
For matching of items in List I with those in List II, which of the following option(s) is/are CORRECT?

(GATE CSE 2025 SET-2)
questions.explanation: Click here for detail solution by gateoverflow

questions.question:
Let \(G_1, G_2 \) be Context-Free Grammars (CFGs) and \(R \) be a regular expression. For a grammar \(G \), let \(L(G) \) denote the language generated by \(G \).
Which ONE among the following questions is decidable?

(GATE CSE 2025 SET-2)
questions.explanation: Click here for detail solution by gateoverflow

questions.question:
Which ONE of the following languages is accepted by a deterministic pushdown automaton?

(GATE CSE 2025 SET-2)
options:
C.

Any language accepted by a non-deterministic pushdown automaton.

questions.question:
Consider a finite state machine (FSM) with one input \( X \) and one output \( f \), represented by the given state transition table. The minimum number of states required to realize this FSM is _________. (Answer in integer)



(GATE CSE 2025 SET-1)

questions.question:
Consider the following deterministic finite automaton (DFA) defined over the alphabet, \( \Sigma = \{ a, b \} \). Identify which of the following language(s) is/are accepted by the given DFA.



(GATE CSE 2025 SET-1)
options:
A.

The set of all strings containing an even number of \( b \)'s.

B.

The set of all strings containing the pattern \( bab \).

C.

The set of all strings ending with the pattern \( bab \).

D.

The set of all strings not containing the pattern \( aba \).
questions.correct_answer:
C.

The set of all strings ending with the pattern \( bab \).
questions.explanation: Click here for detail solution by gateoverflow

questions.question:
Consider the following two languages over the alphabet \(\{a, b, c\}\), where \(m\) and \(n\) are natural numbers:

\(L_1 = \{ a^m b^m c^{m+n} \mid m, n \geq 1 \}\)
\(L_2 = \{ a^m b^n c^{m+n} \mid m, n \geq 1 \}\)

Which ONE of the following statements is CORRECT?

(GATE CSE 2025 SET-1)
options:
B.

\(L_1\) is a context-free language but \(L_2\) is not a context-free language.

C.

\(L_1\) is not a context-free language but \(L_2\) is a context-free language.
questions.correct_answer:
C.

\(L_1\) is not a context-free language but \(L_2\) is a context-free language.
questions.explanation: Click here for detail solution by gateoverflow

questions.question:
Consider the following two languages over the alphabet \(\{a, b\}\):

\(L_1 = \{ \alpha \beta \alpha \mid \alpha \in \{a, b\}^+ \text{ and } \beta \in \{a, b\}^+ \}\)
\(L_2 = \{ \alpha \beta \alpha \mid \alpha \in \{a\}^+ \text{ and } \beta \in \{a, b\}^+ \}\)

Which ONE of the following statements is CORRECT?

(GATE CSE 2025 SET-1)
options:
B.

\(L_1\) is a regular language but \(L_2\) is not a regular language.

C.

\(L_1\) is not a regular language but \(L_2\) is a regular language.
questions.correct_answer:
C.

\(L_1\) is not a regular language but \(L_2\) is a regular language.
questions.explanation: Click here for detail solution by gateoverflow

questions.question:
A regular language \(L\) is accepted by a non-deterministic finite automaton (NFA) with \(n\) states. Which of the following statement(s) is/are FALSE?

(GATE CSE 2025 SET-1)
options:
A.

\(L\) may have an accepting NFA with < \(n\) states.

B.

\(L\) may have an accepting DFA with < \(n\) states.

C.

There exists a DFA with \(\leq 2^n\) states that accepts \(L\).
questions.explanation: Click here for detail solution by gateoverflow

questions.question:
Consider the following context-free grammar \(G\), where \(S\), \(A\), and \(B\) are the variables (non-terminals), \(a\) and \(b\) are the terminal symbols, \(S\) is the start variable, and the rules of \(G\) are described as:

\(S \to aaB \mid Abb\)
\(A \to a \mid aA\)
\(B \to b \mid bB\)

Which ONE of the languages \(L(G)\) is accepted by \(G\)?

(GATE CSE 2025 SET-1)
options:
B.

\(L(G) = \{ a^n b^{2n} \mid n \geq 1 \} \cup \{ a^{2n} b^n \mid n \geq 1 \}\)
questions.explanation: Click here for detail solution by gateoverflow

questions.question:
Consider the following augmented grammar, which is to be parsed with a SLR parser. The set of terminals is \( \{a, b, c, d, \#, @\} \)
\( \begin{aligned} & S^{\prime} \rightarrow S \\ & S \rightarrow S S|A a| b A c|B c| b B a \\ & A \rightarrow d \# \\ & B \rightarrow @ \end{aligned} \)
Let \( I_0=\operatorname{CLOSURE}\left(\left\{S^{\prime} \rightarrow \bullet S\right\}\right) \). The number of items in the set \( \operatorname{GOTO}\left(I_0, S\right) \) is ____

(GATE CSE 2024 SET-2)
questions.explanation: Click here for detail solution by gateoverflow

questions.question:
Let \( L_1 \) be the language represented by the regular expression \( b^* a b^*\left(a b^* a b^*\right)^* \) and \( L_2=\left\{w \in(a+b)^*|| w \mid \leq 4\right\} \), where \( |w| \) denotes the length of string \( w \). The number of strings in \( L_2 \) which are also in \( L_1 \) is ___

(GATE CSE 2024 SET-2)
